title: Quiz: Fuzz Testing Quantum Circuits
description: Test your understanding of fuzz testing techniques for quantum programs.

questions.question: What is the primary goal of fuzz testing?

options.text: To verify correct output for known inputs
To discover unexpected crashes and edge cases through random inputs
To measure code performance
To ensure 100% code coverage

questions.explanation: Fuzz testing generates random or semi-random inputs to discover crashes, edge cases, and unexpected behaviors that might not be found with designed test cases.

questions.question: What is mutation-based fuzzing?

options.text: Testing code for mutations in DNA sequences
Modifying valid inputs slightly to create new test cases
Running tests on mutated quantum states
Changing the source code and checking if tests still pass

questions.explanation: Mutation-based fuzzing takes valid inputs (like working circuits) and makes small modifications to explore nearby input space for bugs.

questions.question: Which parameter value is particularly important to test in quantum circuit fuzzing?

options.text: Only positive integers
Edge cases like 0, pi, inf, and NaN
Only values between 0 and 1
ASCII characters

questions.explanation: Edge values like 0, pi, 2*pi, infinity, and NaN often trigger special code paths or numerical issues that normal values don't expose.

questions.question: What is coverage-guided fuzzing?

options.text: Fuzzing that measures test coverage percentage
Fuzzing that prioritizes inputs that explore new code paths
Fuzzing that only tests covered code
Fuzzing that avoids already-tested paths

questions.explanation: Coverage-guided fuzzing tracks which code paths have been explored and prioritizes inputs that lead to new, unexplored paths.

questions.question: What is differential fuzzing?

options.text: Testing the difference between two versions
Comparing outputs from multiple implementations to find discrepancies
Computing gradients through fuzzing
Testing differential equations

questions.explanation: Differential fuzzing runs the same input on multiple implementations and flags cases where they produce different results, revealing bugs in one or more implementations.

questions.question: Crash minimization attempts to find the smallest input that still triggers a bug.

options.text: True
False

questions.explanation: Crash minimization removes parts of a crashing input while verifying the crash still occurs, ultimately finding the minimal reproducing case for easier debugging.

questions.question: Why is an empty circuit important to include in edge case testing?

options.text: It's the fastest to execute
It tests boundary conditions and null handling
It always produces valid output
It has the highest coverage

questions.explanation: Empty circuits test how the system handles edge cases like zero-length input, which can expose null pointer errors, off-by-one bugs, and improper initialization.
